skill.name: render-automation
skill.description: Automate Render service discovery, deployments, deployment monitoring, and project listing through Rube MCP.
skill.summary: Guide Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Render operations using Rube MCP tools.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution, weak-cryptography, and reconnaissance alerts are false positives from Markdown backticks, YAML field names, and documentation text. The confirmed risk is that the skill directs users to an external MCP endpoint that can authenticate to Render and trigger deployment operations, so publication should include an operational warning.

medium_findings.title: External MCP Endpoint Handles Render Authentication
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to add the Rube MCP endpoint and complete Render authentication through it. This is expected for the skill, but it means account access is delegated to an external integration.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The setup section explicitly requires an external MCP endpoint and an active Render connection. This is a legitimate workflow, but the network and authorization boundary is clear.

medium_findings.title: Deployment Operations Can Modify Render Services
medium_findings.description: The documented workflow can trigger deployments and clear build cache for Render services. These actions can affect production availability if used on the wrong service or without approval.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The tool sequence and pitfalls directly describe deployment-triggering behavior. The operational impact is inherent to the Render API actions being automated.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Alerts Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The Ruby backtick execution alerts correspond to inline code, tool names, parameters, and fenced workflow examples in Markdown. No shell, Ruby, or executable script is present in the scanned file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review shows Markdown documentation only, with backticks used for formatting tool names and parameters. No executable Ruby code or shell invocation exists in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Text Matching False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts align with ordinary prose or metadata, not cryptographic calls. No evidence found of DES, MD5, SHA-1, or other weak algorithm usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are description text, response fields, and a general applicability sentence. They do not contain cryptographic APIs or instructions.

low_findings.title: Static Reconnaissance Alerts Are Operational Documentation
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance alerts occur in deployment monitoring and ID-resolution guidance. No evidence found of host scanning, system inventory collection, or local environment probing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines discuss polling Render deployment status, extracting Render service IDs, and avoiding API rate limits. They are remote service workflow guidance, not system reconnaissance.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Service Connection Required
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill depends on a third-party MCP service for Render operations. Users should verify the MCP server and connection state before authorizing account access.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dependency on the external Rube MCP URL and Render connection workflow is explicit. The pattern is expected, but it expands the trust boundary.

dangerous_patterns.title: Service Deployment Triggering
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents tool calls that trigger Render deployments. This is not malicious, but it is a sensitive operational action.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The deployment tool sequence is explicit and central to the skill. The security concern is operational change control rather than code execution.

content.value_statement: Render operations often require service lookup, deployment triggering, and status checks across several screens. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured workflow for using Rube MCP Render tools safely.
actual_capabilities: Searches Rube MCP tools before running Render workflows.
Lists Render services with name, type, limit, and cursor filters.
Triggers manual Render deployments after resolving a service ID.
Retrieves deployment status and identifies terminal deployment states.
Lists Render projects with cursor-based pagination.
Documents common service ID, deploy ID, and pagination patterns.
limitations: Requires Rube MCP and an active Render connection.
Does not define local scripts or standalone Render API clients.
Cannot bypass Render permissions, rate limits, or authentication requirements.
Deployment actions can affect production services and require human approval.

use_cases.title: Deploy a Known Service
use_cases.description: Find a Render service by name, trigger a deployment, and monitor it until it reaches a final state.

use_cases.title: Audit Service Inventory
use_cases.description: List Render services by type and page through large accounts with cursor-based results.

use_cases.title: Check Project Organization
use_cases.description: List Render projects and confirm how services are grouped before planning changes.

prompt_templates.title: List My Services
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Render automation skill to search current Rube MCP schemas, then list my Render services. Show service names, types, and IDs.

prompt_templates.title: Find a Service by Name
prompt_templates.prompt: Search the current Render tool schema, then find the Render service matching this name: <service name>. Confirm the service ID before suggesting any action.

prompt_templates.title: Deploy and Monitor
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Render workflow to deploy <service name>. Ask for confirmation before triggering the deploy, then monitor until live or failed.

prompt_templates.title: Review Deployment Health
prompt_templates.prompt: For service <service name> and deployment <deploy id>, retrieve the deployment status and summarize timing, result, and recommended next steps.

output_examples.input: List all web services in my Render account.
output_examples.output: A concise service inventory with matching web services, service IDs, pagination status, and a note about any missing next cursor.

output_examples.input: Deploy the staging API and watch the status.
output_examples.output: A confirmation request, then a deployment progress summary showing created, build, update, and final live or failed status.
A project list with project identifiers, pagination notes, and any services that may need separate service lookup.

best_practices: Search Rube MCP tool schemas before each workflow because schemas can change.
Resolve service names to service IDs before any deployment action.
Poll deployment status at reasonable intervals to respect Render rate limits.
anti_patterns: Triggering deployments before confirming the exact service and environment.
Rapid polling that can hit Render API rate limits.
Assuming a service name match is exact without checking returned results.

faq.question: Does this skill run local shell commands?
faq.answer: No. The skill is Markdown guidance for using Rube MCP Render tools.

faq.question: What setup is required?
faq.answer: You need Rube MCP connected and an active Render connection for the Render toolkit.

faq.question: Can it trigger production deployments?
faq.answer: Yes, if the connected Render account has access. Confirm the target service before deploying.

faq.question: Why should tools be searched first?
faq.answer: The skill says to search Rube MCP tools first so current schemas and parameters are used.

faq.question: Does it manage Render projects?
faq.answer: It documents listing projects and handling cursor-based pagination for project results.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium-risk warning because it uses an external MCP service and can trigger deployments.
